messages.text: Outside the court, Bassanio still refused to give the ring. Gratitude pulled one way, Portia's command another.

messages.name: Bassanio
messages.text: Ask for anything else. This token is my wife's first gift and my promise to remain loyal.

messages.name: Antonio
messages.text: My friend, grant him this. His service saved my life, and my love should persuade your wife to forgive.
That pressure broke Bassanio. He sent Gratiano after the doctor with the ring, already feeling regret before it left his hand.

messages.name: Gratiano
messages.text: Good doctor, my lord sends the ring and asks you to dine with him.

messages.name: Portia
messages.text: I accept the ring, not the dinner. Tell him I expected no less generous payment.
Nerissa, still disguised as the clerk, saw her chance to mirror the test. She asked Gratiano for his ring.

messages.name: Nerissa
messages.text: My labor deserves a small reward too. That ring would suit me.

messages.name: Gratiano
messages.text: My wife gave it to me. She would scratch my eyes out if I gave it away.

messages.name: Nerissa
messages.text: Then refuse me. I will report that the clerk was valued below a trinket.
Gratiano disliked appearing ungenerous. He surrendered his ring with a joke, proving even louder vows could bend under vanity.

messages.name: Gratiano
messages.text: Here, take it. If Nerissa grows angry, I will say a beardless clerk bewitched me.
Portia and Nerissa returned toward Belmont with both rings safe in their own keeping. Their trick had become evidence.

messages.name: Portia
messages.text: We will reach home before them and let their own words prove the matter.

messages.name: Nerissa
messages.text: They will swear they defended the rings until death.

messages.name: Portia
messages.text: And we will ask what kind of death wore a lawyer's robe and clerk's cap.
Meanwhile Antonio thanked Bassanio for love shown under pressure. Bassanio answered kindly, but regret pricked him.

messages.name: Bassanio
messages.text: Portia will understand, I hope. The man saved you. How could I refuse?
The question sounded noble. It also sounded like an excuse. A token is small until it carries a promise.
The journey back to Belmont would prove whether husbands could laugh at their own failure and learn from it.
Bassanio told himself the gift was reasonable. A ring could be replaced, but Antonio could not. That argument sounded noble and incomplete.

messages.name: Bassanio
messages.text: I grant the doctor honor, thanks, and money. Must the ring also go?

messages.name: Antonio
messages.text: For my sake, let him have it. I once gave my body for your need; give this token for my release.
Gratiano mocked the matter until Nerissa tested him too. Under pressure, his wit became a door through which his promise escaped.

messages.name: Gratiano
messages.text: If I refuse, I look ungrateful. If I grant it, I go home guilty. That clerk has trapped me neatly.
The women accepted the rings not because metal mattered, but because the token would prove how quickly men excuse themselves.

messages.name: Portia
messages.text: Let them arrive before their stories grow too polished. Fresh regret speaks more honestly.
Portia did not need the ring for wealth. She needed it as a truthful object, a small witness that could not flatter or explain itself.

messages.name: Portia
messages.text: A promise kept only when convenient is not loyal. Let the token travel home before the excuse does.
Nerissa enjoyed the game more openly, but she understood the lesson. Marriage vows deserved memory even when no court stood nearby.

messages.name: Nerissa
messages.text: My husband will make a speech. I intend to make him finish it without the ring.
The ring moved from finger to messenger to disguised wife, gathering meanings at every step. It was no longer jewelry; it was evidence.

messages.name: Gratiano
messages.text: I pray Nerissa values gratitude, because I have spent obedience very badly.
His joke would sound smaller when the clerk became Nerissa again.
